feed_card.headline: Japanese is spoken far faster than Thai, yet both carry about 39 bits of information a second.
teasers: Some languages fire eight syllables a second
Why Vietnamese crams more into every syllable
The speed limit every language seems to share

sections.content: Japanese is spoken far faster than Thai, yet both carry about 39 bits of information a second.

content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><text x='114' y='30' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='600' opacity='0.75' text-anchor='middle'>Syllables / sec</text><text x='293' y='30' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='600' opacity='0.75' text-anchor='middle'>Bits / sec</text><line x1='200' y1='46' x2='200' y2='250' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5' opacity='0.3' stroke-dasharray='6 6'/><line x1='40' y1='250' x2='188' y2='250' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.4' stroke-linecap='round'/><line x1='212' y1='250' x2='372' y2='250' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.4' stroke-linecap='round'/><g stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.55'><rect x='55' y='106' width='40' height='144' rx='6'/><rect x='130' y='165' width='40' height='85' rx='6'/><rect x='245' y='100' width='40' height='150' rx='6'/><rect x='320' y='100' width='40' height='150' rx='6'/></g><line x1='214' y1='100' x2='370' y2='100' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linecap='round'/><text x='292' y='92' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='15' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>all near 39</text><g fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='14' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'><text x='75' y='98'>8.0</text><text x='150' y='157'>4.7</text></g><g fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' font-weight='600' text-anchor='middle'><text x='75' y='270'>Japanese</text><text x='150' y='270'>Thai</text><text x='265' y='270'>Japanese</text><text x='340' y='270'>Thai</text></g></svg>

sections.content: Play a Japanese speaker and a Thai speaker back to back and one sounds like a sprint, the other a stroll. Japanese pours out about 8 syllables a second, Thai fewer than 5. The fast talker seems to be getting more said. But time the information itself and the two converge, each landing near 39 bits a second. The extra syllables mostly buy speed, and the information underneath barely moves.

items: A bit is the surprise in one coin flip, a single yes-or-no settled.
Speaking at 39 bits a second means clearing about forty such coin flips every second.
A Japanese syllable narrows the field to one of roughly 32 likely sounds; a Vietnamese syllable, one of about 256.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><text x='200' y='30' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='600' opacity='0.75' text-anchor='middle'>Bits per syllable</text><line x1='40' y1='115' x2='360' y2='115' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.4' stroke-linecap='round'/><circle cx='80' cy='115' r='6' fill='currentColor'/><circle cx='240' cy='115' r='6' fill='currentColor'/><circle cx='320' cy='115' r='7.5' fill='var(--accent)'/><g fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' font-weight='600' text-anchor='middle'><text x='80' y='96'>Japanese</text><text x='240' y='96'>English</text></g><text x='320' y='96' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>Vietnamese</text><g fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle'><text x='80' y='140'>5 bits</text><text x='240' y='140'>7 bits</text></g><text x='320' y='140' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='13' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>8 bits</text><g fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.6'><text x='44' y='172' text-anchor='start'>sparse</text><text x='356' y='172' text-anchor='end'>dense</text></g></svg>

sections.content: There is no exotic instrument behind the figure. Coupé and his colleagues recorded 170 native speakers across 17 languages, each reading the same 15 short passages at a natural pace. That came to about 240,000 syllables. For every language they timed the syllables, then used Claude Shannon's information theory to measure how much each one narrows down what could come next. A predictable syllable carries few bits; a surprising one carries many. Multiply that density by the speaking rate and you have bits per second. Run all 17 and the totals gather around 39.

content.title: Simple syllables are what let a language sprint
content.body: Why can Japanese go so fast? Its syllables are mostly simple, a consonant plus a vowel: “ka”, “mi”, “to”. English does the opposite. It crams consonants together, so “strengths” lands as a single syllable, and a cluster like that takes real time to say. A syllable that carries more sound carries more information, and it costs more of the clock. Speed and density are two readings of one thing: how a language builds its syllables.

content.title: No language manages to be both fast and dense
content.body: Line the 17 languages up by speed and by density and they slope downward: the faster the syllables, the less each one holds. Spanish and Japanese sprint with light syllables. Vietnamese and Cantonese move slowly and load each syllable hard. What no language manages is both at once, fast syllables that are also information-rich, which would shove the rate well past 39. That corner stays empty. Every language studied sits under the same ceiling.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><g stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' stroke-linecap='round' opacity='0.5'><line x1='60' y1='250' x2='360' y2='250'/><line x1='60' y1='250' x2='60' y2='50'/></g><rect x='250' y='55' width='105' height='72' rx='10' fill='var(--accent)' opacity='0.12'/><text x='302' y='86' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>no language</text><text x='302' y='106' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle'>fast and dense</text><line x1='120' y1='75' x2='323' y2='225' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5' opacity='0.35' stroke-dasharray='6 6'/><g fill='currentColor'><circle cx='120' cy='75' r='5'/><circle cx='98' cy='125' r='5'/><circle cx='188' cy='125' r='5'/><circle cx='248' cy='175' r='5'/><circle cx='308' cy='225' r='5'/><circle cx='323' cy='225' r='5'/></g><g fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.8'><text x='128' y='68' text-anchor='start'>Vietnamese</text><text x='318' y='243' text-anchor='end'>Japanese</text></g><g fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.7'><text x='210' y='283' text-anchor='middle'>Syllables / sec</text><text x='28' y='150' text-anchor='middle' transform='rotate(-90 28 150)'>Bits / syllable</text></g></svg>

sections.content: So the fixed quantity is not a feature of any one language. Nine unrelated families, from Turkic to Austroasiatic, land on the same rate. That points past the tongue, to the mind behind it. The limit looks cognitive: about how fast a person can turn thought into speech and speech back into sense. A language can split the labor differently, more syllables or heavier ones, but the channel it feeds runs near one speed. The faster syllables just carry lighter loads.

content.question: A language that packs a lot of information into each syllable will most likely...
options: be spoken with fewer syllables per second
convey information much faster than other languages
be spoken faster to keep up
have far fewer speakers
content.explanation: Density and speed trade off. A language that loads each syllable heavily tends to be spoken more slowly, which keeps the overall rate near 39 bits a second rather than pushing it higher.

content.question: Across the 17 languages studied, what came out roughly the same?
options: The number of syllables per second
The number of possible syllables
The bits of information conveyed per second
The length of the passages read
content.explanation: Syllable rate and information per syllable both vary widely between languages. Their product, the bits delivered each second, is what clusters, landing near 39 for all of them.

content.question: Japanese is spoken far faster than Vietnamese. Why doesn't a Japanese speaker get more across per second?
options: Japanese speakers pause more between words
Each Japanese syllable carries less information, offsetting the speed
Vietnamese speakers repeat themselves less often
The two are actually spoken at the same rate
content.explanation: The faster syllables are also the lighter ones. Japanese syllables average about 5 bits each and Vietnamese about 8, so the speed advantage is cancelled by a smaller payload per syllable.

content.question: Saying a Vietnamese syllable carries about 8 bits means it...
options: takes eight times longer to pronounce
picks one out of roughly 256 likely syllables
is about eight times louder
always changes the meaning of the word
content.explanation: Eight bits is 2 to the 8th power, or 256 possibilities. A larger pool of likely syllables makes each choice more surprising, which is exactly what carrying more information means.

content.question: Nine unrelated language families all converge near 39 bits a second. What does that most suggest?
options: They share a common ancestor language
The vocal cords have a fixed physical speed
The limit is set by how fast the mind produces and absorbs meaning
International standards fix a translation rate
content.explanation: A shared rate across families that split long ago points away from any one language and toward something common to all speakers: the pace at which a human mind can encode thought into speech and decode it back.

content.question: Why is there no language that is both very fast and very information-dense per syllable?
options: Such a language would run well past the shared rate, and none is found there
Dense languages are always ancient and slow to change
Fast languages carry fewer grammatical rules
No writing system could keep up with it
content.explanation: Speed and density pull against each other. A language high on both would deliver far more than 39 bits a second, and when the languages are plotted that corner sits empty.

content.myth: Some languages are simply more efficient than others.
content.reality: None of the 17 came out ahead. A sparse, quick language and a dense, slow one deliver information at about the same rate, so efficiency is not what sets them apart.

content.myth: A fast-sounding language lets you say more in the same time.
content.reality: The speed is audible but nearly hollow. Each quick syllable carries less, so the meaning arrives at roughly the pace a slower language would set.

content.myth: Every language hits exactly 39 bits a second.
content.reality: It is a cluster, not a clean constant. The 17 scatter around 39 within about one standard deviation, and the figure comes from read-aloud passages, so ordinary conversation may sit a little off it.

content.body: The pattern is clear; its cause is not. The study caught people reading prepared texts, and spontaneous talk, full of pauses and false starts, has never been mapped the same way. Nor is it settled which end sets the limit, producing speech or taking it in. Both could be true at once. The clean 39 may be a real bound of the mind, or just where careful reading happens to land.
items: Does unscripted conversation hold to the same rate?
Is the ceiling set by the speaker or the listener?
Do signed languages, on a different channel entirely, land near it too?

content.label: Coupé C, Oh YM, Dediu D, Pellegrino F. Different languages, similar encoding efficiency: comparable information rates across the human communicative niche. Science Advances 2019;5(9):eaaw2594
CNRS press release: Similar information rates across languages, despite divergent speech rates
Information theory (overview of bits and entropy)
